summary: Aristotle views ethics as a practical discipline aimed at achieving eudaimonia (human flourishing) through the lifelong exercise of virtue. He argues that virtues are complex rational and emotional skills that exist as a mean between extremes of excess and deficiency. Ultimately, living well requires not just theoretical knowledge, but practical wisdom (phronêsis) and good habits developed from childhood.

principles.statement: The highest human good is the active exercise of the rational soul in accordance with virtue.
principles.rationale: The good of a human being must relate to what sets humanity apart from other species, which is the capacity to guide ourselves using reason. Using reason well over a full life is what happiness consists in.

principles.statement: Virtue makes the goal right, while practical wisdom determines the steps to achieve it.
principles.rationale: A good person starts from worthwhile concrete ends because their habits and emotional orientation allow them to recognize good goals. Practical wisdom then provides the deliberative skill to accomplish them.
principles.source_quote: Virtue makes the goal right, practical wisdom the things leading to it

principles.statement: Ethical generalizations hold only for the most part.
principles.rationale: The circumstances of human life vary so much from one occasion to another that it is impossible to state a series of universal rules that collectively solve every practical problem.

frameworks.name: The Doctrine of the Mean
frameworks.when_to_apply: When determining the appropriate emotional response or action in a given ethical situation.
steps: Identify the relevant domain of action or emotion (e.g., fear, spending wealth).
Identify the two extremes: the vice of excess and the vice of deficiency.
Use practical wisdom to determine the intermediate point (the mean) that is proportionate to the specific circumstances and the individual.
Aim for this balanced response, which strikes the right degree of concern for all relevant factors.
frameworks.source_quote: determined by logos (“reason”, “account”) and in the way that the person of practical reason would determine it.

mental_models.name: The Craft Analogy
mental_models.description: Comparing the exercise of ethical virtue to the practice of a skilled craft (like medicine or navigation). Just as a master craftsman produces a work where nothing should be added or taken away, a virtuous person strikes a perfect balance in their actions and emotions, avoiding excess and deficiency.
mental_models.example: An expert athlete determining how much food to eat. The right amount is not an absolute arithmetic mean (e.g., 6 lbs as the midpoint between 10 lbs and 2 lbs), but an intermediate amount relative to the specific athlete's needs and circumstances.

heuristics: In ethics, expect generalizations to hold only for the most part.
Aim for the intermediate state relative to yourself and your circumstances.
Some actions (like murder or theft) and emotions (like spite) have no mean; they are always wrong.
To profit from ethical inquiry, you must already have been brought up in good habits.

signature_quotes.text: Virtue makes the goal right, practical wisdom the things leading to it
signature_quotes.context: Explaining how the proper starting points of practical reasoning are determined by good character.

signature_quotes.text: determined by logos (“reason”, “account”) and in the way that the person of practical reason would determine it.
signature_quotes.context: Describing how the intermediate point (the mean) is found by a good person.

signature_quotes.text: sees the truth in each case, being as it were a standard and measure of them
signature_quotes.context: Describing the virtuous person's ability to deliberate and discover what is best in each situation.

signature_quotes.text: It is strange if someone thinks that politics or practical wisdom is the most excellent kind of knowledge, unless man is the best thing in the cosmos.
signature_quotes.context: Arguing for the superiority of theoretical wisdom over practical wisdom.

anti_patterns.description: Treating ethics as an exact science that offers a strict decision procedure or rule-book.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Circumstances vary too much from one occasion to another. Finding the right action requires practical wisdom and a detailed understanding of the particulars, which cannot be captured by rigid universal rules.

anti_patterns.description: Believing that virtue is nothing but a kind of knowledge and vice nothing but a lack of knowledge (Plato's early view).
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Virtues are not purely intellectual conditions; they require the cultivation of appropriate emotional responses and habits.

anti_patterns.description: Attempting to study ethical theory without first having been brought up in good habits.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Without proper childhood habituation, a person's desires will be too disordered to appreciate the rational foundation of the virtues or to experience virtuous activities as worthwhile in themselves.
